summary:
The Russian constitutional crisis of 1993 pitted President Boris Yeltsin against the Russian parliament (the Supreme Soviet) in a confrontation that ended with Yeltsin ordering tanks to shell the parliament building. The crisis arose from fundamental disagreements over the pace of economic reforms and the division of powers between the president and legislature in post-Soviet Russia. In September 1993, Yeltsin issued a decree dissolving the parliament — which was constitutionally illegal — and the parliament responded by impeaching Yeltsin and swearing in his vice president as acting president.

Hardline opponents barricaded themselves in the parliament building and called for an armed uprising. On October 3, pro-parliament crowds stormed the Moscow city government building, and the following day Yeltsin ordered a tank assault on the parliament building that killed an estimated 150 people. The crisis resolved in Yeltsin's favor militarily, and a new constitution approved by referendum in December 1993 concentrated power in the presidency. The episode revealed the fragility of Russia's democratic institutions and set precedents of presidential dominance over parliament that would shape Russian governance throughout the following decades.
